title: Intersight Server Power and Thermal Telemetry
exclusions: Does not model UCSM-domain-only C-Series rack PSU loss with cisco:ucs:* the way UC-19.1.30 does — keep 19.1.30 for classic UCSM operState and bay counts on sys/rack-unit-…. Does not run the cisco:ucs:stats fan and chassis rate-of-rise runaway path from UC-19.1.34 — add that UC when the failure mode is mechanical cooling inside a blade chassis, not a fleet kW/°C time series from the Intersight cloud. Skips IPMI gap sensors, BMC firmware crash signatures, and host OS power profiles that never touch Intersight MOs. Does not cover Nutanix or VxRail HCI power semantics when those clusters are the primary data path. Does not replace cat-12 networking-equipment power, PoE, or switch thermal. If your estate is UCSM-only with no Intersight-claimed devices, the index in this search stays empty on purpose: enable the Intersight connector first.
dataSources: `index=cisco_intersight` (or your org-standard index) containing continuous power and temperature observations from the Splunk Add-on for Cisco Intersight (`Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight`, [Splunkbase 5953](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5953)). The REST Resource API path materialises Intersight managed objects such as equipment.PsuStats (sourcetype pattern `cisco:intersight:equipmentpsustats` — confirm with `| stats count by sourcetype` in your build), equipment.ChassisStats → `cisco:intersight:equipmentchassisstats`, and processor.EnvironmentStats → `cisco:intersight:processorenvironmentstats`. The Cloud Telemetry or metric platform families (when your TA and entitlement enable them) can appear as `cisco:intersight:psumetricsplatform` and `cisco:intersight:thermalmetricsplatform` instead of, or in addition to, the REST class names. Some deployments also use `cisco:intersight:iwo:metrics` when Intersight Workload Optimizer integration is on. A legacy placeholder name `cisco:intersight:metrics` (sometimes seen in old drafts) is not a single canonical label in current TA 5953 documentation — always verify against the vendor package you installed. Events carry Cisco-style JSON fields: OutputPower, OutputCurrent, Power, Temperature, Dn, and relationship keys such as RegisteredDevice or Moid depending on MO.
app: Install the Splunk Add-on for Cisco Intersight on disk as `Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight` ([Splunkbase 5953](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5953)). Supported on Splunk Enterprise 9.x, 10.x, and Splunk Cloud in current vendor matrices. The TA maps each selected Intersight class to a `cisco:intersight:*` sourcetype; do not assume a one-line “metrics” sourcetype without opening default/props.conf in the build you run.
spl:
`comment("UC-19.1.22 — Intersight Server Power and Thermal Telemetry: fleet power and temperature trends from Intersight-claimed hardware for PDU headroom, ASHRAE-class checking, and sustainability rollups. Union of REST stat and metric-stream sourcetypes; normalises field names; optional PDU and ASHRAE lookups.")`
index=cisco_intersight earliest=-1h@h (sourcetype="cisco:intersight:equipmentpsustats" OR sourcetype="cisco:intersight:psumetricsplatform" OR sourcetype="cisco:intersight:equipmentchassisstats" OR sourcetype="cisco:intersight:processorenvironmentstats" OR sourcetype="cisco:intersight:thermalmetricsplatform")
| eval cidl=lower(coalesce(classId, class_id, ClassId, ""))
| eval st=lower(sourcetype)
| eval power_psu=if(match(cidl,"^equipmentpsustats$") OR match(st,"psumetric"), tonumber(coalesce(OutputPower, output_power, outputPower, "")), null())
| eval power_chass=if(match(cidl,"^equipmentchassisstats$") OR match(st,"chassis"), tonumber(coalesce(Power, power, powerWatts, inputPower, input_power, "")), null())
| eval power_draw_watts=coalesce(power_chass, power_psu, tonumber(coalesce(power, Power, "")))
| eval temp_inlet=if(match(cidl,"^equipmentchassisstats$") OR match(st,"chassis"), tonumber(coalesce(AmbientTemp, ambientTemp, air_inlet, AirInlet, inlet, Inlet, "")), null())
| eval temp_cpu=if(match(cidl,"^processorenvironmentstats$") OR match(st,"processorenviron"), tonumber(coalesce(Temperature, temperature, sensorReading, "")), null())
| eval temp_psu=if(match(cidl,"^equipmentpsustats$"), tonumber(coalesce(Temperature, temperature, "")), null())
| eval peak_temp_c=coalesce(temp_inlet, temp_cpu, temp_psu)
| eval host=toString(coalesce(Dn, dn, moId, MoId, moid, ""))
| rex field=host max_match=0 "(?<chassis_id>sys\\/(?:chassis|rack-unit)-[0-9]+)"
| eval chassis_key=if(isnotnull(chassis_id), chassis_id, host)
| eval ashrae_class=upper(trim(coalesce(ASHRAE_CLASS, ashrae_class, "A2")))
| bin _time span=5m
| stats max(power_draw_watts) as max_w, max(peak_temp_c) as max_temp_c, values(sourcetype) as st_mix by _time, chassis_key
| streamstats global=f current=t window=3 avg(max_w) as ma_w by chassis_key
| lookup intersight_maintenance_windows chassis_key OUTPUT in_maintenance
| where in_maintenance!="1" OR isnull(in_maintenance)
| lookup ashrae_temp_class.csv ashrae_class OUTPUT max_inlet_c, max_cpu_c
| fillnull value=27 max_inlet_c
| fillnull value=100 max_cpu_c
| lookup ucs_chassis_pdu_budget.csv chassis_key OUTPUT pdu_circuit_max_watts, pdu_circuit_id, rack_id
| fillnull value=5000 pdu_circuit_max_watts
| eval pdu_warn=pdu_circuit_max_watts*0.75
| eval pdu_high=pdu_circuit_max_watts*0.85
| eval pdu_crit=pdu_circuit_max_watts*0.95
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    max_w>=pdu_crit OR max_temp_c>max_inlet_c+5 OR max_temp_c>max_cpu_c+3, "critical",
    max_w>=pdu_high OR max_temp_c>max_inlet_c+2, "high",
    max_w>=pdu_warn OR max_temp_c>max_inlet_c, "medium",
    1=1, "low")
| where severity_bucket IN ("medium","high","critical")
| sort 0 -_time, chassis_key
| table _time, chassis_key, rack_id, max_w, ma_w, max_temp_c, pdu_circuit_max_watts, severity_bucket, st_mix
description: Monitors Intersight equipment PsuStats, ChassisStats, and EnvironmentStats class feeds (or the parallel metric stream sourcetypes) for sustained watts and °C, rolled up per chassis or rack key, compares against customer PDU circuit limits and an ASHRAE-class table, and buckets results into medium, high, and critical so capacity teams see breaker saturation and thermal pressure before a trip or throttle — separate from Intersight cond alarms in UC-19.1.19 and firmware compliance in UC-19.1.20.
value: A cross-domain Intersight customer gets one place to see when the fleet is about to run out of headroom on a PDU circuit, when inlet or CPU temperature trends breach an agreed ASHRAE class, and how watts move over time so sustainability reports can show kWh class totals without a second spreadsheet. You gain lead time for breaker upgrades, HVAC coordination, and rack moves, because the MO truth matches what capacity engineers already use in the Intersight control plane, not only domain-local UCSM statistics.
implementation: Enable the Intersight TA stat MO inputs (or metric stream) for Psu, Chassis, and processor environment classes to cisco_intersight, add optional ashrae_temp_class.csv and ucs_chassis_pdu_budget.csv in an app you own, wire intersight_maintenance_windows if you use that pattern, save the spl as a scheduled report, then alert on severity_bucket in high or critical with throttling on chassis_key.
visualization: Line chart: max_w and ma_w per chassis_key over 24h with lines at 75, 85, 95% of pdu_circuit_max_watts. Heatmap: max_temp_c by rack_id. Table: severity_bucket, pdu_circuit_id, st_mix. Single value: fleet kW (sum) from a summary search for ESG reporting.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=true prestats=true
    avg(Performance.Facilities.temperature_celsius) as avg_temp
    max(Performance.Facilities.temperature_celsius) as max_temp
    sum(Performance.Facilities.power_load_watts) as sum_watts
  from datamodel=Performance
  where nodename=Performance.Facilities
  by _time span=5m
| stats first(avg_temp) as avg_inlet, first(max_temp) as peak_inlet, first(sum_watts) as total_w by _time
| where isnotnull(total_w) OR isnotnull(peak_inlet)
| eval fleet_kW=round(total_w/1000,3)
dataModelAcceleration: If you CIM-map Intersight environmental samples into the Performance data model, enable acceleration for Performance in Settings → Data models → Performance → Edit → Acceleration and set the summary range to your capacity review window. Use the cimSpl variant for fleet rollups; keep the raw MO spl for field-level troubleshooting when the TA renames a JSON key.

references.title: ASHRAE — Thermal Guidelines for Data Processing Environments

knownFalsePositives: HVAC scheduled setback or economizer handover that lifts inlet a few degrees for a planned window before returning — compare BMS tickets, not only Splunk. Phase rebalance on a PDU during a datacenter audit can move a large share of kW to another leg and trip the 85% warning on one chassis_key with healthy servers — check the electrical one-line, not a hardware RMA. Intersight telemetry stream gaps while IMC or BMC firmware is staged during a rolling refresh produce empty buckets; gate pages on at least two bad bins. Short cold-aisle door or containment breach from cleaning crews can raise inlet for tens of minutes without a part fault; add a facilities timestamp before you wake hardware engineering.
hardware: Cisco UCS B-Series (examples B200 M5, B200 M6, B200 M7, B480 M5) and C-Series rack servers (C220 M6, C240 M6/M7, C480 M5/M6), UCS X210c M6/M7 in UCS X9508 and related X-Fabric or IFM-cabled builds, and C-Series HyperFlex-class nodes where Intersight is the day-to-day control plane. Covers UCS managed through Fabric Interconnects and Intersight Direct (IMM) stand-alone servers that are Intersight-claimed. Supports Cisco Intersight SaaS, Intersight connected appliance, and on-prem Intersight virtual deployment models that expose the same REST equipment and processor stats to the Splunk add-on. Always confirm a device family actually emits PsuStats or aggregate Power in your subscription before you promise a kW line per serial.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight from Splunkbase ([5953](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5953)) on a Heavy Forwarder (or the tier that runs modular inputs) and on Search Heads for field knowledge, saved searches, and lookups. On-disk name is exactly Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight; the “Cisco Intersight Add-on” name on Splunkbase is the same product.
• Intersight (SaaS, connected appliance, or private virtual install your contract lists) is the control plane. This UC is for the customer who needs cross-domain, fleet-aggregated power and thermal time series, rack-level or circuit-level budget headroom, and ESG style watt-hour style trending across UCSM-managed, IMM standalone, and mixed estates — not a single-domain UCSM XML story alone. UC-19.1.30 and UC-19.1.34 stay on Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs and cisco:ucs:* for classic UCSM rack PSU posture and blade chassis runaway; keep those when your authoritative feed is the UCSM poll. Use UC-19.1.22 when Splunk is fed from the Intersight side so one index can see many domains and you can line up RegisteredDevice / MO identity with the same Intersight workflow your capacity team already trusts.
• Network: TLS egress from the input host to the Intersight API hostname your org documents (intersight.com for typical SaaS, or your on-prem appliance FQDN). Proxy and inspection policies must allow the REST paths the TA version lists in its setup notes; 401/403 are usually key scope, clock skew, or proxy MITM, not a Splunk search bug.
• RBAC: create a dedicated API key with read access to the equipment and processor stat MO families you enable. Minimum practical scope: view inventory and the stat classes in Step 1. Avoid handing the Splunk key Organisation Administrator. Document which Intersight Account it belongs to; mixing lab and production keys in one index is a common evidence defect.
• License: stat MO polling at three- to five-minute effective cadence on large fleets is measurable in GB/day. Budget before you widen MO filters to “all equipment.* without a class list”.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection**
(1) Install the TA on the forwarder that will run the modular input and on Search Heads. In Apps → Manage Apps, confirm the green Enabled state. Pin a build your security team reviewed; major TA bumps can rename optional JSON keys.
(2) Intersight key — In the Intersight UI, create an API key (KeyId + Secret) under your account or org settings. Store it through the add-on’s credential workflow or passwords.conf via the UI, never a cleartext secret in Git. Map the key to an Account stanza the modular input references. A read-only or auditor-style role is enough for stat reads if your subscription allows; many teams use a “Splunk integration” custom role with read to equipment and processor resource APIs.
(3) Resource API (REST) poll path (recommended for parity with MO names) — Enable inputs that materialise Cisco’s equipment and processor stat managed objects. In current TA families, the MO classes equipment.PsuStats, equipment.ChassisStats, and processor.EnvironmentStats are the three families this UC leans on for continuous watts and °C, alongside any parallel metric platform stream your build exposes. The Splunk sourcetype is commonly cisco:intersight: plus the class string with dots removed and lowercased: cisco:intersight:equipmentpsustats, cisco:intersight:equipmentchassisstats, cisco:intersight:processorenvironmentstats. Always run | stats count by sourcetype in your org after the first two cycles and reconcile with the exact strings your TA version’s default/props.conf and documentation show — field names in this use case are defensive with coalesce() to absorb camelCase, snake_case, and a few renames.
(4) File-driven pattern for local/inputs.conf — Copy the vendor stanza names from $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight/default/inputs.conf, then override in local/inputs.conf. A structural illustration you must align to the shipped file is:
```ini
# Pattern only: reconcile every key to Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight default/inputs.conf for YOUR version
# [intersight://<vendor_stanza_name>]
# account = <passwords.credential_stanza>
# index = cisco_intersight
# disabled = 0
# mo_class_filter = equipment.PsuStats,equipment.ChassisStats,processor.EnvironmentStats
# interval = 300
# (endpoint / sourcetype_prefix / additional keys per vendor)
```
(5) Metric or telemetry stream path (optional alternate) — Some TA 3.x deployments expose the Cloud Telemetry Service family as cisco:intersight:psumetricsplatform, cisco:intersight:thermalmetricsplatform, or similar. If your stats count by sourcetype shows these instead of the REST stat class names, keep them in the saved search OR list; the coalesce() block in the spl field is written for that split.
(6) Intersight Workload Optimizer (IWO) metrics — If you are licensed for IWO and the TA shows cisco:intersight:iwo:metrics, you may parallel that stream for host-level workload context; this UC’s primary still expects equipment stat MOs for hardware watts and temperature.
(7) Index — Bind to a governed name such as cisco_intersight. Role srchIndexesAllowed must include it for operators.
(8) Volume — Expect multiple events per chassis per poll when you sum PSUs and include CPU environment rows; 10–30 KB per minute per hundred servers is a rough planning number until you measure license_usage.log.

**Step 2 — Create the search and alert**
(1) Open Search, set an appropriate time range (Last 4 hours for first test), and paste the full spl from this use case. If the first line uses the backtick comment macro and your search head does not define it, add a one-line macros.conf in your app: [comment(1)] with definition = "" or remove the first line; the rest of the search stands alone.
(2) Pipeline in plain terms — The base index/sourcetype clause unions every deployment flavour (REST stat classes, platform metric stream names) so a single saved search works from customer to customer. eval and coalesce() normalise field names between JSON shapes. classId checks separate chassis aggregate power, per-PSU output watts, and CPU package temperature. A rex on Dn (or a MO id fallback) gives a chassis_id for rack rollups. The search bins time, then uses streamstats for a short moving average of watts as a stabiliser against your PDU or lookup budget. Optional lookup rows for ashrae_temp_class and ucs_chassis_pdu_budget (you create) layer ASHRAE A2 style bands and the breaker headroom; optional intersight_maintenance_windows (pattern exists in sibling Intersight UCs) can suppress planned work. The case() block yields severity_bucket (critical, high, medium) tied to 95% of circuit budget, ASHRAE +5°C, and the medium tier for early warning. The closing where leaves low-only rows out of a paging queue when you set thresholds conservatively in production.
(3) Alerting — Schedule every 5 minutes with earliest=-2h@m and latest=now (tune to poll cadence). Throttle 1 to 2 hours on chassis_key to avoid re-pages during one cooling transient. Runbook owner example: “Datacenter Capacity Engineering” in the alert description.
(4) If lookup files are not ready day one, the sample search still runs: place pdu_circuit_max_watts in the organisation CSV, or rely on the fillnull default until facilities publishes circuits.
(5) The fence below is the same logic as the top-level spl; paste into an alert, not a retyped variant.

```spl
`comment("UC-19.1.22 — Intersight Server Power and Thermal Telemetry: fleet power and temperature trends from Intersight-claimed hardware for PDU headroom, ASHRAE-class checking, and sustainability rollups. Union of REST stat and metric-stream sourcetypes; normalises field names; optional PDU and ASHRAE lookups.")`
index=cisco_intersight earliest=-1h@h (sourcetype="cisco:intersight:equipmentpsustats" OR sourcetype="cisco:intersight:psumetricsplatform" OR sourcetype="cisco:intersight:equipmentchassisstats" OR sourcetype="cisco:intersight:processorenvironmentstats" OR sourcetype="cisco:intersight:thermalmetricsplatform")
| eval cidl=lower(coalesce(classId, class_id, ClassId, ""))
| eval st=lower(sourcetype)
| eval power_psu=if(match(cidl,"^equipmentpsustats$") OR match(st,"psumetric"), tonumber(coalesce(OutputPower, output_power, outputPower, "")), null())
| eval power_chass=if(match(cidl,"^equipmentchassisstats$") OR match(st,"chassis"), tonumber(coalesce(Power, power, powerWatts, inputPower, input_power, "")), null())
| eval power_draw_watts=coalesce(power_chass, power_psu, tonumber(coalesce(power, Power, "")))
| eval temp_inlet=if(match(cidl,"^equipmentchassisstats$") OR match(st,"chassis"), tonumber(coalesce(AmbientTemp, ambientTemp, air_inlet, AirInlet, inlet, Inlet, "")), null())
| eval temp_cpu=if(match(cidl,"^processorenvironmentstats$") OR match(st,"processorenviron"), tonumber(coalesce(Temperature, temperature, sensorReading, "")), null())
| eval temp_psu=if(match(cidl,"^equipmentpsustats$"), tonumber(coalesce(Temperature, temperature, "")), null())
| eval peak_temp_c=coalesce(temp_inlet, temp_cpu, temp_psu)
| eval host=toString(coalesce(Dn, dn, moId, MoId, moid, ""))
| rex field=host max_match=0 "(?<chassis_id>sys\\/(?:chassis|rack-unit)-[0-9]+)"
| eval chassis_key=if(isnotnull(chassis_id), chassis_id, host)
| eval ashrae_class=upper(trim(coalesce(ASHRAE_CLASS, ashrae_class, "A2")))
| bin _time span=5m
| stats max(power_draw_watts) as max_w, max(peak_temp_c) as max_temp_c, values(sourcetype) as st_mix by _time, chassis_key
| streamstats global=f current=t window=3 avg(max_w) as ma_w by chassis_key
| lookup intersight_maintenance_windows chassis_key OUTPUT in_maintenance
| where in_maintenance!="1" OR isnull(in_maintenance)
| lookup ashrae_temp_class.csv ashrae_class OUTPUT max_inlet_c, max_cpu_c
| fillnull value=27 max_inlet_c
| fillnull value=100 max_cpu_c
| lookup ucs_chassis_pdu_budget.csv chassis_key OUTPUT pdu_circuit_max_watts, pdu_circuit_id, rack_id
| fillnull value=5000 pdu_circuit_max_watts
| eval pdu_warn=pdu_circuit_max_watts*0.75
| eval pdu_high=pdu_circuit_max_watts*0.85
| eval pdu_crit=pdu_circuit_max_watts*0.95
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    max_w>=pdu_crit OR max_temp_c>max_inlet_c+5 OR max_temp_c>max_cpu_c+3, "critical",
    max_w>=pdu_high OR max_temp_c>max_inlet_c+2, "high",
    max_w>=pdu_warn OR max_temp_c>max_inlet_c, "medium",
    1=1, "low")
| where severity_bucket IN ("medium","high","critical")
| sort 0 -_time, chassis_key
| table _time, chassis_key, rack_id, max_w, ma_w, max_temp_c, pdu_circuit_max_watts, severity_bucket, st_mix
```

**Step 3 — Validate**
(1) In the Intersight UI (same org as the API key): navigate Operate → select a device → Server, then Power or Power statistics (wording depends on form factor) and read live watts for a known server. Compare magnitude and direction to a timechart in Splunk in the same window.
(2) API spot-check — Using an admin session, open the Intersight equipment stats paths your tenant documents (Cisco publishes PsuStats under /api/v1/equipment/PsuStats) and match OutputPower to one indexed event with head 1. If the UI and API agree but Splunk lags, widen earliest to include two full poll periods.
(3) intersight-curl — If your runbook already uses Cisco’s intersight-curl helper, run the documented GET for PsuStats and compare fields to the indexed event.
(4) IMC fallback (standalone) — For IMM-attached C-Series, the local IMC can still show power supply readouts; use that only to explain odd rows, not as a second mandatory ingest.
(5) This validation path is different from 19.1.19 (alarms) and 20 (firmware) because you are proving numeric **sensor** and **watt** paths, not Code strings or version equality.

**Step 4 — Operationalise**
(1) RACI — Accountable: Datacenter Capacity Engineering for thresholds and circuit budgets. Consulted: Facilities for breaker labels. Informed: Sustainability for quarterly kWh. Splunk platform owners for index and acceleration.
(2) Dashboard layout — Row 1: line chart of max_w and the streamstats-based moving average per chassis_key over 24h with lines at 75, 85, 95% of pdu_circuit_max_watts. Row 2: heatmap of max_temp_c by rack_id. Row 3: top-N highest CPU or inlet readings. Row 4: single value of fleet kW (sum) for ESG, driven from a summary or datamodel to avoid 90d raw scan.
(3) Lookups — Maintain ashrae_temp_class.csv with ashrae_class, max_inlet_c, and optional max_cpu_c. Keep ucs_chassis_pdu_budget.csv keyed on chassis_id with pdu_circuit_id, pdu_circuit_max_watts, and rack_id. Reconcile with electrical one-line quarterly.
(4) Weekly capacity review: discuss medium rows for trend, not one-off. Quarterly PDU re-allocation when GPU or AI nodes land.

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting**
• Zero results — Check modular input enabled; btool the stanza. Verify the API key is not expired. Confirm processor.EnvironmentStats is supported on your Intersight tier — Essentials can differ from Advantage for cadence; narrow span in timechart if the doc says 1m in Essentials and 30s in Advantage. If you chose a telemetry stream input, verify Cloud Telemetry is entitled for the device group.
• Too many alerts — Filter to a list of business-critical chassis_id values, add intersight_maintenance_windows rows for HVAC test nights, and require two consecutive 5m bins over threshold before a page so sub-minute transients from VM bursts are ignored.
• Stale data — Intersight Cloud telemetry can have one-minute or thirty-second resolution depending on tier; a flat line that does not change for hours may be a dead poll or a true steady load — check _indextime minus _time and TA logs.
• Field drift after upgrade — Rerun fieldsummary for one hour; extend the coalesce() list with any new names from release notes. Do not delete historical props until index migration completes.

Additional operator notes: When two sourcetypes both carry watts for the same chassis, the saved search preferentially uses chassis-level Power over per-PSU rows via coalesce order to limit double counting; if you only have PsuStats, the 5m max still shows peak leg draw. For SaaS Intersight, use the hostnames your tenant shows. For on-prem, place the base URL in the TA account, not a hardcoded eval. For sustainability, multiply time-weighted kW with care: missing buckets are not the same as zero watts. If Essentials tier limits stat families, remove unavailable MOs from the mo_class filter instead of hoping they appear. Throttle on two bad bins, not one. When REST and metric streams both run, values(sourcetype) in the result tells you which path supplied the row after a restart.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on how much electricity your shared computer racks use and how warm they are getting over time, across all the sites your team manages in one place. That way you can add power or fix cooling before a breaker flips or things slow down, and you can show leaders clear totals when they ask about energy use.
